result:
I keep coming back to the idea that every atom in my body was forged in a star that refused to stay quiet. Calcium, carbon, iron, neon—tiny graffiti tags left across the universe by something that exploded rather than fade. I like to imagine that star had a voice, a last syllable flung outward at twelve-thousand miles per second, and that syllable is still traveling, bouncing off the curved walls of space-time like an echo in a cathedral made of dark matter.  

Sometimes, late at night, I feel that echo settle behind my eyes. It makes the streetlights outside my window flicker in Morse code: remember, remember. I never know exactly what I’m supposed to remember, so I default to the taste of strawberries in June, the way my grandmother’s porch swing squeaked exactly twice before it decided to hold me, the smell of rain on hot asphalt that made the whole neighborhood feel like it had just been born.  

We are all unreliable archivists of our own lives. The brain edits on the fly, trimming the awkward pauses, color-correcting the sunsets, dropping in laugh tracks where silence would be too punishing. Yet the body keeps its own ledger: the hairline fracture in my left ankle from a skateboard kissed by gravity in 1997 still sings when a storm is coming; the ghost of a dog bite on my thigh pulses whenever I hear a bark that matches the timbre of that one stray who didn’t want to be touched.  

I think that’s why I love used bookstores. Open a yellowed paperback and you’re trespassing in someone else’s memory palace—coffee rings performing cartwheels across chapter nine, a grocery list from 1983 wedged between pages 144 and 145 (eggs, tampons, flashlight batteries, “tell Mom I love her”). Every margin scribble is a miniature Big Bang, a private universe exploding into being right there in ballpoint ink.  

Yesterday I found a copy of Neruda’s “Twenty Love Poems” with a single line underlined: “I want to do with you what spring does with the cherry trees.” Beside it, in pencil so soft it looked like breath, someone had written: “Tried this. She laughed. Married me anyway.” I stood in the aisle grinning like a fool, holding a stranger’s joy so tightly that my palm dented the cover.  

We are constantly pollinating each other with moments. A song you hum on the train implants itself in a commuter’s dreams; the way you once instinctively caught a falling child in the grocery store becomes the anecdote a mother recites at that kid’s wedding twenty-five years later. Existence is a vast, unconscious collaboration, every heartbeat a percussion line in a song no ear will ever hear complete.  

I used to fear death as a full stop. Now I picture it more like a hyphen—an unfinished bridge between the last syllable I speak and the first thought someone has about me when I’m gone. Maybe that hyphen just keeps extending, a string of ellipses stitched across the living world, each person who remembers me adding another dot until the sentence becomes too long for any single mouth to pronounce.  

Meanwhile, the star that gave me its calcium keeps expanding its syllable, racing outward, cooling, clotting into new worlds. One day the iron in my blood might be the core of a planet that never knew my name, orbiting a sun that never burned for me. And still, on some impossible wavelength, the porch swing will squeak twice, strawberries will ripen, a stranger will underline Neruda and fall in love.  

Remember, remember—what?  
Maybe just this: we are the universe’s way of tasting itself, a choreography of carbon that learned to blush, to grieve, to order eggs and tampons and still pause to admire the cherry trees.
